story:
On the last night of summer, after watching Secrets of a Sidekick, Tommy drifted to sleep and stepped into a city of heroes and villains. On a park bench, a towering figure in white spandex appeared behind him and said, "The movies are all wrong." Spider bites, ooze, explosions—none of that makes a hero. "Then how?" Tommy asked. The man leaned close and whispered, "Training."

Tommy followed the white-clad hero through quiet, almost mundane proofs of strength—righting a car, pulling a stump—until an explosion rocked the city. Hovering above the chaos was a masked villain calling himself The Detonator, taunting, "Snow White," clearly mocking the man in white. He threatened to bring down a twenty-story office tower across from a school in session. Sirens wailed. Without a fight, the villain triggered the blast. The building tipped. People screamed. While everyone searched the sky for a showdown, Tommy spotted the man in white already planted on the school roof, arms raised. With impossible gentleness, he caught the falling tower and held it suspended over the roofline, steady as a bridge. He pried open sideways windows and eased terrified workers out while children and teachers evacuated below. When Tommy asked why he hadn't gone after the villain, the hero answered, "Your strength is best used to hold others up, not take people down." He added, "Your training with me is complete," and bounded off.

At dusk, a flicker on the horizon swelled and wrapped Tommy in light. A slender man in orange spandex appeared and said, "Follow me." In a blur of orange flashes, they stopped a dozen crimes. Then came the real test. Mirage, a quantum prodigy turned criminal, snapped a red energy dome around them. The field mirrored a hero's powers to calibrate, then shifted to green: ten minutes in which forcing an exit would free the trapped but strip their powers forever, while waiting would let Mirage siphon those powers for himself. Others had frozen, trying to save what they had. The orange hero knelt, calm. He sprinted. The barrier burst. Mirage gained nothing; the speedster lost his gift. Using the last seconds before it ebbed away, he still finished their final call—carrying a baby boy to the hospital in time. When Tommy asked how he'd chosen so fast, the speedster said, "Your speed in taking action will always be secondary to your speed in decision-making, so decide who you are before the moment arrives." Then he jogged away, ordinary and unafraid.

Near dawn, Tommy met a third figure—tall, silent, dressed in black with glowing eyes. They walked for blocks as the man's gaze swept buildings, streets, sewers, sky—seeing beneath surfaces. On Tower Road they found dozens of clogged storm drains, an overfilled water tower, and, hidden under brush, the hoverboards of The Detonator and Mirage. A bomb with thirty minutes left ticked beneath the tank. The man in black studied it, clipped the right wire, reversed the covert pump that had filled the tower, and rewired the hoverboards to home to their owners and hard-lock. Moments later, the boards lifted from the brush, sought their paired users, clamped on, and ferried the villains helplessly to the police station. "Your ability to see what's going on behind the scenes can often defuse the threat and sometimes even change its course," he said. Back at the park bench, all three heroes nodded to Tommy and vanished into sunrise.

Tommy woke renewed. At school a new kid stumbled on the stairs, and a bully named David stuck out a foot, sending him sprawling. Tommy's fist clenched—until he saw through David's meanness to the hurt beneath it. His mind moved faster than his fist. He walked past the bully, reached out, and used his strength to lift the new student. "What's your name?" he asked. "Peter," the boy said.
